acceptance_targets: [secondary_character_naming_instability_chapter_02] Agnes's roommate and the Room 110 resident each use a single consistent name in Chapter 2 matching all other chapters.
[RED-01] Ch2's first Lola passage does not mention both silver hair and gold crucifix. At most one physical trait from Ch1's introduction may appear, and only in passing.
[CON-01] Agnes's roommate is named consistently in every chapter. No instance of 'Mrs. Nowak' or 'Dorothy' remains in the manuscript when referring to this character.
[CON-03] The Room 110 moaning patient has a single consistent name throughout, or any name change is accompanied by a narrative acknowledgment of a new patient.

findings.acceptance_test: Agnes's roommate and the Room 110 resident each use a single consistent name in Chapter 2 matching all other chapters.
findings.evidence: FINAL_NOVEL.md:266 'Agnes's roommate, a woman named Dorothy who hadn't spoken in eleven months and received nutrition through a PEG tube.' FINAL_NOVEL.md:308 references the Room 110 resident with a different name than Ch1.
findings.problem: Agnes's roommate is 'Dorothy' here but was 'Mrs. Nowak' in Ch1 and will become 'Doris Okonkwo' in Ch8. The Room 110 resident's name also shifts from Ch1's 'Delvecchio.'
findings.rewrite_direction: At FINAL_NOVEL.md:266, replace 'Dorothy' with the chosen permanent name for Agnes's roommate. At FINAL_NOVEL.md:308, replace the Room 110 resident's name with the chosen permanent name and ensure gender consistency.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch2's first Lola passage does not mention both silver hair and gold crucifix. At most one physical trait from Ch1's introduction may appear, and only in passing.
findings.evidence: Ch1 L17: 'Lola's silver hair was loose on her shoulders...The gold crucifix at her throat caught the light from the television'; Ch2 L244: 'her silver hair freshly brushed — Tammy would've done it on the evening round — and her gold crucifix catching the last daylight through the blinds'
findings.problem: Lola's two-trait descriptor pairing (silver hair + gold crucifix) is re-established in Ch2 one chapter after Ch1's full introduction at L17. The reader already has both details from the previous chapter.
findings.rewrite_direction: Remove or replace the silver-hair-plus-gold-crucifix pairing in Ch2 L244. Enter the scene through Lola's behavior, posture, or the specific emotional register of her greeting — not through traits the reader learned last chapter. A single identifying detail (e.g., the tremor, the recliner, the game-show glow) is sufficient.

findings.acceptance_test: Agnes's roommate is named consistently in every chapter. No instance of 'Mrs. Nowak' or 'Dorothy' remains in the manuscript when referring to this character.
findings.evidence: Ch1 L41: Agnes's roommate is 'Mrs. Nowak'; Ch2 L266: Agnes's roommate is 'a woman named Dorothy who hadn't spoken in eleven months and received nutrition through a PEG tube'; Ch8 L1571: 'Her roommate, Doris Okonkwo, eighty-three, was five years into Alzheimer's'; Ch8+ uses 'Doris' consistently (L1577, 1705, 1721, 1815, 1914, 2110); Ch18 L4361/4423/4465/4595 uses 'Doris'
findings.problem: Agnes's roommate in Room 108A is given three different names in the first 8 chapters: Mrs. Nowak (Ch1 L41), Dorothy (Ch2 L266), and Doris Okonkwo (Ch8 L1571). All three descriptions refer to a woman with advanced dementia in Agnes's room. The name stabilizes as 'Doris' from Ch8 onward but the Ch1 and Ch2 names are contradictory.
findings.rewrite_direction: Standardize Agnes's roommate name to 'Doris Okonkwo' throughout. In Ch1 L41, replace 'Mrs. Nowak' with 'Doris' or 'Mrs. Okonkwo.' In Ch2 L266, replace 'Dorothy' with 'Doris.' Verify all subsequent references maintain the same name.

findings.acceptance_test: The Room 110 moaning patient has a single consistent name throughout, or any name change is accompanied by a narrative acknowledgment of a new patient.
findings.evidence: Ch1 L11: Room 110 patient is 'Mr. Delvecchio was ninety-one and contracted into a permanent C-shape'; Ch2 L308: Room 110 patient is 'Mrs. Davila moaned through the night'; Ch4 L685: Room 110 patient is 'Mrs. Delacroix — produced the same low, continuous murmur'
findings.problem: The Room 110 moaning patient changes name three times: Mr. Delvecchio (Ch1 L11), Mrs. Davila (Ch2 L308), and Mrs. Delacroix (Ch4 L685 onward). All three are described as producing the same continuous moaning/murmuring sound. Ch2's 'Mrs. Davila' appears only once and contradicts both the Ch1 and Ch4+ names.
findings.rewrite_direction: Standardize the Room 110 patient name. If Mr. Delvecchio dies or is transferred between Ch1 and Ch4 (plausible in a nursing home), make that transition explicit with a brief narrative mention. If the same patient is intended throughout, pick one name — 'Mrs. Delacroix' is used most consistently (Ch4-Ch18) — and update Ch1 and Ch2 accordingly. In Ch2 L308, replace 'Mrs. Davila' with the chosen name.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This is a dark comedy about elderly agency, institutional failure, and moral compromise in a setting most fiction treats as a punchline or a tragedy but never both. The risks the novel must take: render the comedy of octogenarians as pharmaceutical distributors without condescending to them or turning them into lovable rogues; make Carmen's escalating complicity fully legible without moralizing about it; show the bodily realities of aging — incontinence, tremor, cognitive decline, falls, the smell of a body that doesn't leave a building — with unsentimental specificity; render the economic desperation of both healthcare workers and uninsured buyers without turning either into social-issue abstractions; give the moral ambiguity of the ring genuine weight by refusing to resolve it into either 'they were wrong' or 'they were heroes.' The novel must trust readers to sit with complexity. When in doubt between too raw and too safe, choose raw. A nursing home that smells like potpourri is a nursing home written by someone who's never been in one.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism for bodily functions, aging, economic shame, institutional dysfunction, or the realities of nursing home care is a craft failure. The novel's comedy and humanity depend on rendering what polite fiction glosses over: what it smells like to clean someone who can't clean themselves, what it costs to count coins for a bus fare, what oxycodone sells for per milligram on the secondary market, what a concussion looks like on an 88-year-old skull, what it sounds like when someone with Parkinson's tries to hold a coffee cup steady, what Burt's dementia actually looks like up close. If the prose flinches from these specifics, the novel loses its authority.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the default for all dialogue. Uncontracted forms appear only for emphasis, character-specific formality (Agnes's low contraction level), Carmen under extreme stress reverting to formal speech, or deliberate register choices. In a contemporary New Jersey nursing home, stiff diction is the anomaly, not the norm.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate high-intensity scenes with reflective interiority. Vary scene count: single-scene chapters for maximum compression, three-scene chapters for range. Comic chapters (3, 9) should feel structurally different from quiet character studies (7, 8, 12, 15), which should feel different from crisis chapters (11, 16, 17). Follow a confrontation with a reflection, an escalation with a domestic scene, a comic set piece with an elegiac one. If two consecutive chapters share the same rhythm, one needs redesigning.
prose_style_profile.diction: Grounded and specific. Drug names (oxycodone, alprazolam, hydroxychloroquine), nursing procedures (med pass, pill count, charting, fall assessment), body realities (incontinence, tremor, laceration, scalp bleed). No purple prose, no literary performance. The style should feel like someone who works with their hands is telling you the truth about what she saw.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, varying by POV character. Carmen chapters are warm and sensory, anchored in the body and the immediate environment. Harry chapters are precise and calculating, with attention to numbers and angles. Agnes chapters are clinical and compressed. Lola chapters are winding and emotionally layered, with embedded memories. Earl chapters are spare and weighted, with silence doing as much work as language. Marcus chapters are kinetic, with physical movement and code-switching. Tammy chapters are flat and observational, her trained eye cataloguing what doesn't add up.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied by chapter and POV. Carmen chapters favor medium sentences with occasional long runs when she's anxious. Harry chapters are short and clipped. Comic set pieces (chapters 3 and 9) move between fast inventory and slow domestic beats. Quiet chapters (7, 12, 15) slow down and let space onto the page. No chapter should feel rhythmically identical to the one before it.
